title: Mango & Cardamom Overnight Oats
description: Rolled oats soaked overnight in yogurt and milk, layered with ripe mango and perfumed with ground cardamom. A no-cook, grab-from-the-fridge breakfast that tastes like mango lassi in a bowl.

steps.text: In a jar or bowl, stir together the oats, curd, milk, honey and cardamom powder until well combined. The mixture should be loose — it will thicken considerably overnight as the oats absorb the liquid.
Press a layer of half the diced mango into the oat mixture, then spoon the remaining mixture on top. Cover tightly and refrigerate for at least 6 hours, or overnight.
In the morning, give the oats a stir. If they feel too thick, loosen with a splash of cold milk. Top with the remaining fresh mango, sliced banana and chopped almonds before serving cold.

substitutions.substitute: plant-based coconut yogurt for a dairy-free version
substitutions.notes: skip the milk or use coconut milk too

substitutions.substitute: jaggery syrup (dissolve in a little warm water) or sugar
substitutions.notes: vegans use jaggery or maple syrup

substitutions.substitute: ripe banana or fresh pineapple chunks
substitutions.notes: any soft ripe fruit works

substitutions.substitute: roasted cashews or peanuts for a budget option

culturalNote: Overnight oats became mainstream in health-conscious American kitchens in the 2010s — the mango-cardamom pairing is a natural South Asian riff that makes this dish feel at home on both continents.
indianKitchenAdaptation: This needs zero adaptation for Indian kitchens — dahi (thayir/curd), Alphonso or Totapuri mangoes, and elaichi powder are all pantry staples. Use chilled full-fat dahi for a creamier result. Soaking in a stainless-steel dabba with a lid works perfectly.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Kadai-Roasted Granola
description: Oats, coconut, almonds and cashews toasted together in a kadai with melted coconut oil and jaggery until fragrant and golden — an Indian-kitchen take on American granola that needs no oven. Eat it with cold curd, a splash of coconut milk, or straight from the jar.

ingredients.name: desiccated coconut or grated fresh coconut

steps.text: Heat a wide kadai on medium-low flame. Add the oats, almonds, cashews and coconut together — do not add any oil yet. Dry-toast, stirring continuously, for 5 minutes until the oats begin to smell nutty and turn faintly golden.
Add the sesame seeds and toast for another 2 minutes, still stirring, until the seeds begin to pop. Lower the flame to its lowest setting before the next step.

Pour the melted coconut oil over the oat mixture and stir well so every piece is coated. Sprinkle in the grated jaggery, cardamom and salt. Stir constantly for 3–4 minutes — the jaggery will melt, coat the oats and caramelise slightly. Take off the heat the moment the mixture smells like toffee.

Spread the hot granola onto a clean plate or tray in a thin layer and let it cool completely — it will harden and clump as it cools. Do not stir while cooling or it will not cluster. Once fully cool, break into pieces and store in an airtight jar for up to 2 weeks.
Serve over cold curd, coconut milk or plain milk with fresh mango or banana slices on top.

substitutions.substitute: honey or sugar (note: honey makes it non-vegan)
substitutions.notes: use 2 tbsp honey or sugar

substitutions.substitute: any neutral cooking oil
substitutions.notes: coconut oil adds a pleasant coconut aroma

substitutions.substitute: peanuts for a budget version
substitutions.notes: peanuts add a denser crunch

substitutions.substitute: puffed rice (murmura) for a lighter, crispier texture

culturalNote: Granola is a fixture of American health-food culture since the 1970s. This stovetop version was developed to recreate the crunchy clusters without an oven, making it accessible to Indian kitchens worldwide.
indianKitchenAdaptation: This recipe is designed for the Indian kitchen — a wide kadai on a gas flame replaces the oven, jaggery replaces maple syrup, and coconut oil is readily available. Almonds and cashews are cheaper per kilo at Indian grocery stores than anywhere else. The finished granola keeps well in a steel dabba.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Banana Peanut Butter Smoothie Bowl
description: Frozen banana blended thick with peanut butter and a splash of coconut milk, poured into a bowl and loaded with toppings. It eats like ice cream for breakfast but delivers real staying power from peanut protein and banana carbs.

ingredients.name: coconut milk
ingredients.notes: add more if needed to blend smoothly

steps.text: The night before, peel ripe bananas, slice them into coins and freeze in a zip-lock bag or covered container. Frozen banana is what gives the bowl its thick, creamy consistency — fresh banana will not work as well.

Add the frozen banana pieces, peanut butter and coconut milk to a blender. Blend on high for about 60 seconds, stopping to scrape the sides as needed. The mixture should be very thick — thicker than a smoothie you drink. Add honey if the bananas are not sweet enough.
Pour the thick blend into a chilled bowl immediately. Working quickly before it melts, arrange toppings: oats for crunch, fresh banana slices, and crushed peanuts. Serve at once.

substitutions.substitute: cold water or cold milk
substitutions.notes: water gives a lighter flavour; dairy milk gives a creamier result

substitutions.substitute: almond butter or cashew butter
substitutions.notes: changes the allergen profile; reduce quantity to 1.5 tbsp as tree-nut butters are richer

substitutions.substitute: mango chunks (frozen) for a tropical mango smoothie bowl

culturalNote: Smoothie bowls rose from Brazilian açaí bowls and became a mainstream American breakfast trend in the 2010s. The peanut butter version is the most protein-rich and wallet-friendly riff.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Freeze ripe Robusta or Cavendish bananas — available everywhere in India — overnight in a steel container. Use homemade peanut chutney (groundnut paste) or store-bought creamy peanut butter available at most supermarkets. A regular mixie (Indian blender jar) works fine; blend in short pulses to protect the motor when the mixture is very thick.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Avocado Toast with Chilli Oil
description: Ripe avocado mashed onto toasted bread with lemon, salt and a generous drizzle of red-chilli-infused oil. Simple enough to make in five minutes, deeply satisfying when the avocado is perfectly ripe.

ingredients.name: olive oil or any neutral oil

steps.text: Toast the bread slices until golden and firm — in a toaster, on a tawa on medium flame, or in a pan with a scrape of oil. The toast must be crisp enough to hold the mash without going soggy.
Halve the avocados, remove the stones and scoop the flesh into a bowl. Add lemon juice, salt and black pepper. Mash roughly with a fork — some texture is pleasant, so do not over-mash into a puree.

In a tiny pan or ladle, warm the olive oil on low heat for 30 seconds. Add the red chilli powder and let it sizzle for 5 seconds to bloom the spice. Remove from heat immediately before the chilli burns.
Spread the avocado mash generously over the hot toast slices. Drizzle the chilli oil over each piece, scatter spring onion, and serve at once — the toast softens quickly once topped.

substitutions.substitute: hummus (chickpea paste) for a budget and avocado-unavailable swap
substitutions.notes: skip the mashing step; spread hummus directly

substitutions.substitute: coconut oil, gingelly oil or any neutral cooking oil
whole-wheat roti (tawa-toasted) or multigrain bread for a more Indian-friendly base
lime juice — same quantity

culturalNote: Avocado toast became a defining dish of millennial food culture in the United States and Australia during the 2010s. The chilli oil drizzle is its spice-lover's upgrade.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Avocados are now available in most Indian metro supermarkets and in the south during March–July. Look for Hass avocados (dark-skinned); they should yield to gentle pressure when ripe. Toast bread directly on a medium tawa rather than in a toaster. The chilli oil can be made hotter with Kashmiri red chilli powder for colour and guntur powder for heat. Gingelly oil (nallennai) adds a distinctive South Indian note.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Masala Omelette Pav
description: A spiced two-egg omelette folded with onion, tomato, green chilli and coriander, served tucked into soft pav buns. Mumbai's most satisfying roadside breakfast, achievable in under 15 minutes on a tawa.

steps.text: Crack 2 eggs into a bowl (for one portion). Add half the onion, tomato, green chilli and coriander leaves. Sprinkle in turmeric, chilli powder, black pepper and salt. Beat together with a fork until everything is well combined — this is your masala egg mixture for one omelette.

Heat 1 tsp oil in a tawa or flat pan on medium-high flame until shimmering. Pour in the egg mixture and spread it into a round. Let it cook undisturbed for about 1 minute until the edges firm up and the bottom is set but the top is still slightly runny.

Fold the omelette in half, press gently, and cook for another 30 seconds per side. The centre should be cooked through but still moist. Slide onto a plate. Repeat with the remaining mixture for the second omelette.

While the omelettes are cooking, toast the pav buns cut-side down in butter on the same tawa for 30–60 seconds until golden. Tuck one omelette into each pair of pav buns. Serve immediately — the combination of hot omelette and buttered pav is the whole point.

substitutions.substitute: besan (gram flour) batter for a vegan masala 'omelette' — see besan chilla
substitutions.notes: use 0.5 cup besan whisked with water and the same spices

substitutions.substitute: whole-wheat roti or sandwich bread if pav is unavailable
substitutions.notes: pav buns are ideally soft and slightly sweet

substitutions.substitute: oil for a dairy-free finish

culturalNote: Masala omelette pav is the cornerstone of Mumbai's Irani café breakfasts and street carts alike — cheap, fast, and satisfying in a way that no other egg dish quite matches.
regionalVariation: Pune versions often add more onion and skip tomato; Kolhapuri-influenced carts use fiery sukka chilli powder instead of the milder Kashmiri variety.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Tamago Gohan (Japanese Egg on Rice)
description: A raw egg cracked over steaming-hot freshly cooked rice, seasoned with a drizzle of soy sauce and a few drops of sesame oil. Japan's simplest breakfast harnesses the heat of the rice to gently cook the egg into a silky, custardy coating.

ingredients.name: short-grain or sona masoori rice, cooked hot
egg, very fresh, at room temperature

steps.text: Cook the rice in a pot or pressure cooker (2 whistles) with 1.5 cups water until each grain is tender and slightly sticky. The rice must be scorching hot — freshly cooked — for the egg to cook through safely from residual heat.

Serve the hot rice immediately into a bowl. Crack the egg directly over the mound of rice. Beat the egg briefly with chopsticks or a fork right on top of the rice, working fast so the heat of the rice cooks the egg into a creamy, slightly runny layer.
Add the soy sauce, sesame oil and sliced spring onion. Stir and fold the rice through a few more times, mixing the egg coating into every grain. Eat immediately — the dish does not wait.

substitutions.substitute: tamari for a gluten-free version, or salt with a squeeze of lemon
any neutral oil — sesame oil adds the distinctive aroma
substitutions.notes: skip if unavailable

substitutions.substitute: silken tofu crumbled over rice with soy sauce for a vegan version

culturalNote: Tamago gohan is Japan's quintessential comfort breakfast, eaten daily by millions across all ages. The dish depends on ultra-fresh eggs and hot rice — the pairing is centuries old.
regionalVariation: Tokyo versions often add a touch of dashi stock. Kansai homes sometimes add a drizzle of mirin or a small knob of butter.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Use ponni, sona masoori or any short-grain rice cooked in a pressure cooker (2 whistles, 1.5 cups water per cup of rice). The key is to use the rice the moment the pressure drops — tamago gohan is ruined by warm, not-hot rice. Soy sauce is widely available at Indian supermarkets; substitute with a pinch of salt and a drizzle of nallennai (gingelly oil) for an entirely local version. Use the freshest eggs you can buy — ideally country eggs (naatu muttai).
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Egg-in-a-Hole Toast
description: A circle is cut from a bread slice, the bread is fried in butter, and a fresh egg is cracked into the hole to cook right in the pan. A British breakfast classic where the bread toasts in its own butter while the egg sets inside the frame.

ingredients.name: green chilli sauce or hot sauce (optional)

steps.text: Using a round cookie cutter, the mouth of a glass or a knife, cut a circle from the centre of each bread slice. The circle should be about 6–7 cm wide. Keep the cut-out round — you will toast it alongside.
Melt half the butter in a frying pan or tawa on medium flame. Place two bread frames and their cut-out rounds into the pan. Let the bread fry for about 90 seconds until golden on the underside.

Flip both the frames and the rounds. Reduce the flame to medium-low. Crack one egg into each hole in the bread frame. Season immediately with salt and pepper. Cover the pan and cook for 2–3 minutes — the whites should be fully set while the yolk remains runny. For a hard yolk, give it an extra 1–2 minutes.
Slide two frames with their toasted rounds onto each plate. Serve immediately with hot sauce on the side. The cut-out rounds are perfect for dipping into a still-runny yolk.

substitutions.substitute: ghee (desi ghee gives a rich, nutty flavour) or any oil for a dairy-free version
thick multigrain bread or sourdough bread for more structure

culturalNote: Known by many names — toad-in-a-hole bread, egg toast, one-eyed egg — this dish appears across British, American and European breakfast traditions. It is loved for its simplicity and the satisfying visual of the egg set inside the toast frame.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Use any thick bread available — Britannia or local bakery bread works well. Replace butter with ghee (nei/ghee) for a richer flavour familiar to Indian palates; ghee also tolerates higher heat without browning too quickly. Use a kadai lid or plate to cover the pan while the egg sets. Add green chilli sauce or sprinkle red chilli flakes over the yolk for an Indian heat upgrade.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Banana Oat Pancakes
description: Light, naturally sweet pancakes made from ripe mashed banana, wheat flour and oats, flipped to golden perfection on a tawa. The banana acts as both sweetener and moistening agent, cutting the need for much added sugar.

steps.text: Mash the bananas in a large bowl with a fork until only small lumps remain. The riper the bananas — ideally very spotty — the sweeter and more fragrant the pancakes will be.

Add the eggs, milk and vanilla to the mashed banana. Whisk together until smooth. Fold in the wheat flour, oats and baking powder. Stir briefly until just combined — a few lumps are fine and preferable to over-mixing, which makes the pancakes tough. Rest the batter for 3 minutes.

Heat a tawa or flat frying pan on medium flame. Add a tiny amount of butter and swirl to coat. When the butter just begins to foam, pour in roughly 3 tbsp of batter per pancake — leave space between each. Cook until bubbles appear on the surface and the edges look set, about 2–2.5 minutes.

Flip each pancake with a flat spatula and cook the second side for 1.5–2 minutes until golden. The pancake should spring back when gently pressed. Repeat for the remaining batter, adding a little butter before each batch.
Stack the finished pancakes on a warm plate. Drizzle with honey and serve immediately — or top with sliced banana for double banana impact.

substitutions.substitute: rice flour or ragi (finger millet) flour for a gluten-free or healthier version
substitutions.notes: increase milk by 2 tbsp if batter feels too thick

substitutions.substitute: coconut milk or any plant-based milk for a dairy-free version
1 tbsp flax seeds soaked in 3 tbsp water for 5 minutes (flax egg)
substitutions.notes: skip egg for a vegan option; pancakes will be slightly denser

substitutions.substitute: jaggery syrup or sugar

culturalNote: Banana pancakes are a beloved traveller's breakfast across South-East Asia and a staple of American diner menus. The oat version became popular in health-food circles as a higher-fibre, naturally sweetened alternative to classic buttermilk pancakes.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Ripe desi bananas (Mysore/Ney Poovan/Robusta) work beautifully here — they mash smoother and are sweeter than supermarket Cavendish. Use whole-wheat atta, which gives a slightly nuttier result than maida. Cook on a cast-iron tawa on medium-low gas flame; Indian gas flames can be very hot, so reduce to avoid burning the bottom before the inside sets. Jaggery syrup (melt vellam with a splash of water) makes an excellent local topping in place of honey.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Flaxseed Breakfast Pudding
description: Ground flax seeds soaked in coconut milk overnight until they swell into a thick, pudding-like texture — rich in omega-3 fats and fibre. Sweet, creamy and dairy-free, topped with ripe banana and a drizzle of jaggery syrup.

ingredients.name: flax seeds, ground (or whole)
ingredients.notes: ground absorbs liquid better; grind in mixie if needed

steps.text: If using whole flax seeds, grind them coarsely in a mixie or coffee grinder — ground flax absorbs liquid faster and creates a smoother pudding. Whole seeds can be used but take longer to swell and will have a crunchier texture.
Whisk the ground flax seeds, coconut milk, jaggery, cardamom and vanilla together in a bowl until the jaggery dissolves. The mixture will look thin at this stage — that is correct.

Divide the mixture into two jars or containers, cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, or overnight. The flax seeds absorb the liquid and swell into a thick, spoonable pudding with a texture similar to tapioca or chia pudding.
Remove from the fridge and stir well. If the pudding is too thick, loosen with a splash of water or coconut milk. Top with sliced banana and chopped almonds. Serve cold.

substitutions.substitute: cold water, cold oat milk or any plant milk
substitutions.notes: use full-fat coconut milk for a richer pudding

substitutions.substitute: honey (no longer vegan) or sugar
substitutions.notes: 2 tsp sugar per serving is plenty

substitutions.substitute: No direct substitute — flax seeds provide the gelling; without them this recipe does not set

culturalNote: Flaxseed pudding is an omega-3-rich alternative to chia pudding that uses a seed widely available in Indian grocery stores as 'alsi' (Hindi) or 'ali vidhai' (Tamil). It is popular in health-conscious Western breakfast culture.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Flax seeds (alsi/ali vidhai) are sold at every kirana store and health-food shop in India — they are far cheaper here than in Western markets. Grind a batch in a mixie dry-jar and keep in the fridge. Coconut milk (thengai paal) from a carton or freshly squeezed from grated coconut both work. Cardamom (elaichi/elakkai) and jaggery make this taste like a South Indian dessert for breakfast. Refrigerate in a steel container overnight.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Mexican Breakfast Quesadilla
description: Flour tortillas filled with scrambled egg, melted cheese, capsicum and onion, then toasted in a pan until the outside is crisp and the cheese inside has melted into the egg. A quick Mexican morning meal that eats equally well at any hour.

steps.text: Heat 1 tsp oil in a frying pan on medium flame. Add the onion, capsicum and green chilli. Saute for 3–4 minutes until the onion is soft and the capsicum is slightly tender but still has a little bite.

Whisk the eggs with salt, pepper and coriander leaves. Pour over the softened vegetables. Scramble gently, pushing the mixture from the edges to the centre, and remove from heat when the eggs are just barely set — they will finish cooking inside the quesadilla. Add the diced tomato and fold through.

Heat a clean tawa or griddle on medium flame — no oil needed. Place one tortilla on it. Scatter half the grated cheese over one half of the tortilla, then spoon half the egg filling over the cheese. Fold the empty half over to make a half-moon shape.

Press the folded quesadilla lightly with a spatula and cook for 90 seconds until the underside is golden and slightly charred in spots. Flip carefully and cook the other side for another 60–90 seconds until golden and the cheese is melted and bubbling at the seam.

Remove from the tawa, rest for 30 seconds, then cut into triangles with a knife or pizza cutter. Repeat for the second quesadilla. Serve hot with tomato ketchup, green chilli sauce or plain curd on the side.

substitutions.substitute: large whole-wheat roti or maida paratha for an Indian kitchen swap
substitutions.notes: works beautifully on a tawa

substitutions.substitute: paneer, crumbled and pan-fried, for a desi version
substitutions.notes: paneer does not melt like cheese, so scatter it evenly

substitutions.substitute: corn kernels or peas for a sweeter filling

culturalNote: Quesadillas are among the oldest pre-Hispanic preparations of Mexico, originally made with corn tortillas and cheese. The breakfast egg version is a modern everyday evolution found across Mexican home kitchens.
regionalVariation: Oaxacan versions use the local cheese quesillo (string cheese) and black beans; northern Mexico variations add minced meat or chorizo.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Flour tortillas are available at most Indian metro supermarkets. Alternatively, use store-bought whole-wheat rotis or fresh maida chapatis — they crisp up identically on a tawa. Amul processed cheese or any grated mozzarella works as the melting cheese. A medium-hot gas tawa is ideal; no oil is needed on the tawa for the toasting step as the oil inside the filling prevents sticking. Serve with green chutney or tomato ketchup as dipping sauces.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Fruit and Nut Lassi Bowl
nativeTitle: ਫਲ ਅਤੇ ਮੇਵੇ ਵਾਲਾ ਲੱਸੀ ਕਟੋਰਾ
description: Thick churned curd sweetened with honey, poured over ripe mango and banana, then crowned with slivers of almond, pistachio and a whisper of cardamom. Punjab's celebratory lassi tradition meeting the modern breakfast bowl.

ingredients.name: thick fresh curd (hung for 15 minutes or full-fat)

steps.text: Whisk the curd with honey and cardamom powder until smooth and creamy. If using refrigerator-cold curd, the bowl will have a pleasant thick-milkshake consistency. For a thicker result, hang the curd in muslin cloth over a bowl for 15 minutes before using to drain excess whey.
Divide the sweetened curd between two wide bowls. Arrange the mango slices and banana coins over the curd — alternate them for colour contrast.

Scatter the slivered almonds, chopped pistachios and raisins over the fruit. Finish with an extra drizzle of honey if desired. Serve cold and immediately — the fruit will release juices into the curd as it sits, which is delicious but changes the texture.

substitutions.substitute: coconut cream blended with a squeeze of lemon for a dairy-free version
jaggery syrup or sugar
apple, pineapple or any seasonal fruit in India
roasted peanuts or cashews for a budget-friendly nut option

culturalNote: Lassi is Punjab's signature beverage — sweet or salted, churned from curd — and forms the backbone of Punjabi hospitality. The bowl format turns this drink into a full breakfast, drawing on the same tradition of curd, fruit and dry fruits that appears in Punjabi festive spreads.
regionalVariation: Amritsari lassi is renowned for its thick layer of malai (cream) floated on top. Delhi dhabas serve rose-flavoured lassi. This bowl version is a modern home-kitchen riff.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Besan Toast (Gram Flour Toast)
description: Bread slices dipped in a spiced besan batter — turmeric, green chilli, coriander — and pan-fried until golden and crisp. A Maharashtrian breakfast staple that resembles a savoury French toast and fills the kitchen with an irresistible aroma.

ingredients.name: water
ingredients.notes: adjust to make a thin coating batter

steps.text: In a wide bowl, whisk the besan, water, turmeric, red chilli powder, salt and cumin seeds into a smooth, lump-free batter — it should have the consistency of thin pancake batter, thick enough to coat a bread slice without dripping off immediately. Stir in the chopped onion, green chilli and coriander leaves.

Heat 1.5 tbsp oil in a wide tawa or frying pan on medium flame. While the oil heats, dip two bread slices into the batter one at a time, letting them soak for 3–4 seconds per side so the batter penetrates slightly.

Lay the coated slices in the hot oil. Cook for 2–2.5 minutes per side until deep golden and crisp — the besan forms a savoury, slightly crispy crust. Flip gently with a flat spatula. Fry the remaining slices in the second batch, adding the remaining oil before they go in.
Drain on a plate lined with kitchen paper or newspaper. Serve immediately with tomato ketchup, green chutney or a small bowl of curd. The toast loses its crunch quickly, so eat at once.

substitutions.substitute: rice flour plus 1 tbsp cornflour for a crispier, gluten-free coating
substitutions.notes: the texture will be lighter and crunchier

substitutions.substitute: potato slices (parboiled) or thick raw banana slices for a no-bread version
a pinch of black pepper for less heat

culturalNote: Besan toast is a cousin of the Mumbai Irani café scene, where besan-coated snacks have long been popular. It is comfort food for generations of Maharashtrian children — quick to make, filling and boldly spiced without being expensive.
regionalVariation: Pune home cooks sometimes add a pinch of ajwain (carom seeds) to the batter for a more fragrant result; Gujarat versions skip chilli and add a little sugar.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Akuri (Parsi Scrambled Eggs)
description: Soft, buttery scrambled eggs cooked with onion, tomato, fresh ginger and green chilli until just set — loose and creamy, never dry. The Parsi community's signature egg dish, eaten with warm pav or roti at breakfast or any time hunger calls.

steps.text: Crack the eggs into a bowl and beat them lightly with a fork — just enough to break the yolks. Do not over-beat. Season with salt and turmeric and set aside.

Melt the butter in a frying pan or kadai on medium flame. Add the cumin seeds and let them sizzle for 20 seconds. Add the onion and cook for 4–5 minutes, stirring, until soft and translucent — not browned.

Add the ginger and green chilli and fry for 1 minute. Add the tomato and cook for 3–4 minutes, pressing down, until the tomato softens and merges into the onion. The mixture should smell fragrant and the raw tomato smell should be gone.

Reduce the flame to the lowest setting. Pour in the beaten eggs and add half the coriander leaves. Using a wooden spoon or rubber spatula, push the egg mixture slowly from the edges to the centre in wide, lazy circles. The key to akuri is patience — let the eggs cook slowly on low heat, in soft, large, creamy folds.

Remove from heat when the eggs are just barely set but still slightly glossy and moist — they will continue cooking for a moment in the residual heat. Finish with the remaining fresh coriander. Serve immediately with toasted pav or buttered bread.

substitutions.substitute: ghee for a richer version or oil for a lighter one
substitutions.notes: butter is traditional and gives akuri its characteristic softness

substitutions.substitute: soft silken tofu, crumbled, for a vegan scramble — add a pinch of kala namak for a sulphurous egg-like aroma
soft whole-wheat roti or lavash

culturalNote: Akuri is the bedrock of Parsi breakfast culture, tied intimately to the Zoroastrian community of western India. Unlike Indian scrambled eggs that are often cooked dry, akuri is deliberately kept soft and custardy — a technique more akin to Persian-influenced cooking.
regionalVariation: Some Parsi cooks add a pinch of nutmeg or a splash of cream for a richer version. A dryer, more spiced variation called 'lagan nu achaar eggs' includes pickle brine.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Honey Curd & Fruit Parfait
description: Layers of thick strained curd, golden honey, toasted oats and ripe seasonal fruit built up in a glass or bowl. A Greek-inspired parfait that is as pretty as it is easy, and works with whatever fruit is in season.

ingredients.name: thick curd or hung curd (Greek-style yogurt)
ingredients.notes: strain through muslin cloth for 30 minutes to thicken

steps.text: If time allows, hang the curd in a muslin cloth over a bowl in the fridge for 30 minutes. This drains the whey and gives a much thicker, creamier base. The curd should be dense enough to hold its shape briefly when spooned. Stir in the cardamom powder if using.

Toast the oats in a dry pan on medium-low flame, stirring for 3–4 minutes until they turn golden and smell nutty. Spread them on a plate to cool — they will crisp as they cool. Do not skip this step, as raw oats taste chalky against the curd.

Build the parfait in two glasses or wide bowls: spoon a layer of thick curd, add a layer of diced mango and banana, scatter some toasted oats and a few almonds, then repeat until the glass is full. Finish with a final layer of fruit and a generous drizzle of honey over the top.
Scatter the chopped pistachios over the honey as a final garnish. Serve cold and immediately. If making ahead, keep the toasted oats separate and add them right before serving to preserve the crunch.

substitutions.substitute: coconut cream whipped with a pinch of salt for a dairy-free version
jaggery syrup, dates syrup or sugar
roasted peanuts or sesame chikki (broken) for a budget-friendly crunch
pineapple, apple or any seasonal fruit

culturalNote: Greek yogurt with honey (yiaourti me meli) is served across Greece at every hour — breakfast, dessert, or afternoon snack — and is considered both a morning ritual and a near-sacred pairing in Cretan cuisine.
regionalVariation: Cretan versions use thyme honey and walnuts. Turkish versions layer in rose jam. This South Asian interpretation uses mango and cardamom for a natural tropical-Mediterranean crossover.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Strain any full-fat Indian dahi (thayir/curd) through a clean muslin or thin cotton cloth tied over a vessel for 20–30 minutes to approximate Greek-style yogurt. Indian full-fat dahi from brands like Amul or local dairies works best. Honey is widely available; or use palm jaggery dissolved in warm water for a more desi sweetness. Alphonso or Kesar mango slices make this parfait extraordinary between April and June.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen
